content.overview:
Diarrhoeal disease is the second leading cause of death in children under 5 globally. Cholera is a severe, acute diarrhoeal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. Both are closely linked to unsafe water, poor sanitation, and inadequate hygiene. Both are entirely preventable.

Q: How do I prepare ORS at home if sachets are not available?
A: Emergency home ORS: 1 litre boiled water + 6 level teaspoons of sugar + ½ teaspoon salt. This is a temporary measure — get proper ORS sachets as soon as possible.

Q: Is diarrhoea always an infection?
A: No. Diarrhoea can also be caused by lactose intolerance, IBS, food intolerance, medications, or anxiety. If diarrhoea is recurrent without infection signs, consult a doctor.

Q: Should I avoid all dairy when I have diarrhoea?
A: In adults, you can continue a normal, light diet. Curd (yogurt with live cultures) may actually help gut recovery. Breastfeeding should continue for infants.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
Diarrhoea with severe dehydration is a medical emergency, especially in children and the elderly. Give ORS immediately and call 112 if the person is very ill. This information is for awareness and first-line response only.
types: Key facts:
Diarrhoea kills approximately 1.6 million people per year, mostly children under 5.
In India, diarrhoea accounts for approximately 13% of under-5 deaths.
Diarrhoea is the leading cause of malnutrition in children under 5.
Most deaths from diarrhoea are caused by dehydration — preventable with ORS.
Rotavirus is the most common cause of severe childhood diarrhoea globally.
Causes:
Viral: Rotavirus (most common in children), norovirus
Bacterial: E. coli, Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Vibrio cholerae
Parasitic: Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Entamoeba histolytica (amoebic dysentery)
Route: Faecal-oral — contaminated water, food, or hands
Key facts:
Cholera is an acute diarrhoeal disease caused by Vibrio cholerae.
Severe cholera causes profuse watery diarrhoea (rice-water stools) and vomiting, leading to rapid, severe dehydration.
Without treatment, severe cholera can kill within hours.
With treatment, case fatality should be below 1%.
India has periodic cholera outbreaks, especially post-floods and in areas with poor sanitation.
Symptoms:
Sudden onset of profuse watery diarrhoea (described as "rice-water" — pale and watery)
Vomiting
Leg cramps
Rapid dehydration (can lead to shock and death if untreated)
Many infected people have mild or no symptoms but can still spread the disease.
Severe dehydration signs (medical emergency):
Sunken eyes
Dry mouth and extreme thirst
No urination
Muscle cramps
Weak/rapid pulse
Lethargy or unconsciousness
→ CALL 112 and give ORS immediately
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
symptoms: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
warning_signs: Profuse watery diarrhoea (possible cholera) — especially during monsoon or flood situations
Child is unconscious, extremely lethargic, or has no urine for 8+ hours
Suspected cholera case: isolate patient and contact local health authority
Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
risk_factors: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
prevention: Do not drink from unknown sources during outbreaks or flood situations
Use a toilet — open defecation spreads diarrhoeal pathogens widely
Safe disposal of child's faeces — in toilet
Wash hands with soap before eating or preparing food
Wash hands with soap after using the toilet or cleaning a child
This single action can reduce diarrhoeal disease by 40–48%
Avoid raw fruits and vegetables from unknown sources during outbreaks
Rotavirus vaccine: Free under India's Universal Immunisation Programme — protects against the most common severe diarrhoea in children.
Oral Cholera Vaccine (OCV): Used in outbreak situations and high-risk areas. Available at government facilities.
environmental_control: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
vaccination: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
diagnosis: Not separately specified or not applicable in the available source text for this topic.
treatment_awareness_only: The cornerstone of treatment is rehydration:
ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution):
Give ORS after every loose stool
ORS sachets are available FREE at all government health centres
Prepare: 1 sachet in 1 litre of clean/boiled water
Give small sips frequently — do not force large amounts at once
Continue breastfeeding infants during diarrhoea
Continue normal feeding as soon as tolerated (do not starve)
Zinc supplementation:
Give zinc 20 mg/day for 14 days for children with diarrhoea
Reduces severity and duration, and prevents recurrence for 2–3 months
Available free at government hospitals under IMNCI programme
When to seek medical care:
Diarrhoea not improving with ORS after 24–48 hours
Blood in stool (dysentery)
Signs of dehydration (sunken eyes, no urination, lethargy)
High fever
Child cannot keep fluids down
Suspected cholera (very profuse watery diarrhoea, rice-water appearance)
What NOT to do:
Do NOT give anti-diarrhoeal medicines (loperamide) to young children without doctor advice — can mask severity
Do NOT stop feeding — starvation worsens recovery
Do NOT take antibiotics without doctor's advice
Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Seek medical care for severe, persistent, worsening, or red-flag symptoms. Use emergency number 112 for life-threatening situations.
india_specific_context: Diarrhoea cases spike during monsoon season (July–October).
Post-flood water contamination is a major cause of cholera outbreaks.
IDSP (Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme) monitors diarrhoea outbreaks.
Report suspected cholera outbreaks to local health authorities.
